paper_title: LLaTiSA: Towards Difficulty-Stratified Time Series Reasoning from Visual Perception to Semantics

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Comprehensive understanding of time series remains a significant challenge for Large Language Models (LLMs). Current research is hindered by fragmented task definitions and benchmarks with inherent ambiguities, precluding rigorous evaluation and the development of unified Time Series Reasoning Models(TSRMs). To bridge this gap, we formalize Time Series Reasoning (TSR) via a four-level taxonomy of increasing cognitive complexity. We introduce HiTSR, a hierarchical time series reasoning dataset comprising 83k samples with diverse task combinations and verified Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories. Leveraging HiTSR, we propose LLaTiSA, a strong TSRM that integrates visualized patterns with precision-calibrated numerical tables to enhance the temporal perception of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Through a multi-stage curriculum fine-tuning strategy, LLaTiSA achieves superior performance and exhibits robust out-of-distribution generalization across diverse TSR tasks and real-world scenarios. Our code is available at https://github.com/RainingNovember/LLaTiSA.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Time series data serve as fundamental modality for capturing the temporal dynamics of complex systems, underpinning wide range of critical applications, including medical diagnostics (Xie et al., 2025a), financial valuation (Xu et al., 2025), and industrial condition monitoring (Wang et al., 2025b). Interpreting real-world temporal dynamics necessitates Time Series Reasoning (TSR): deriving comprehensive understanding of time series by grounding analysis in numerical evidence, perceiving salient patterns, and incorporating contextual information to support challenging tasks such as decision-making or forecasting. *Work done at internship at Amap, Alibaba. Corresponding author. Recent studies have demonstrated that Large Language Models (LLMs) can be effective for timeseries understanding (Xie et al., 2025b) and provide interpretable reasoning to further enrich traditional time series analysis (Zhang et al., 2025a). Despite these advancements, building unified TSRMs faces two critical bottlenecks: (1) The lack of formalized taxonomy for reasoning tasks. Existing suites like TSQA (Kong et al., 2025) or BEDTime (Sen et al., 2025) either present mapping of traditional analytical tasks (e.g., forecasting and classification) to text formats or focus on realizing scenario-agnostic series patterns. In contrast, domain-specific datasets like Engine-QA (Wang et al., 2025b) and Heartcare-Bench (Xie et al., 2025a) tend to be numerically-insensitive, primarily relying on the semantic relationships between multiple series. (2) Reliability deficits in existing benchmarks. Beyond insufficient contextual information, current benchmarks are often hampered by semantic ambiguities in label definitions and low-fidelity question-answer pairs (Guan et al., 2025). To facilitate the systematic development and rigorous assessment of TSRMs, it is essential to formalize unified definitions of TSR and construct reliable benchmarks that comprehensively model capabilities across diverse scenarios. Building on the aforementioned limitations, we posit that cultivating reliable TSR capabilities mirrors multi-stage cognitive process: transitioning from point-level numerical grounding to serieslevel perception, facilitating high-level semantic interpretation, and ultimately enabling context-aware generation. Guided by this philosophy, we formalize systematic taxonomy that decomposes TSR into four levels of increasing complexity (L1L4), as shown in Figure 1(a). Leveraging this taxonomy, we introduce HITSR (Figure 1(b)), hierarchical time series reasoning dataset comprising 83k samples that span the foundational TSR tasks across the first three levels (L1-L3). All samFigure 1: (a) Illustration of the four-level TSR taxonomy and HITSR dataset. Each incorporates simple TSR case with increasing difficulties. (b) brief introduction to HITSR datasets, containing three levels of TSR scenarios with diverse subtasks. ples in HITSR are constructed with unambiguous ground truths and verifiable reasoning chains. This paper focuses on levels L1-L3, since we find that even advanced TSRMs struggle significantly on these foundational tasks1, suggesting that mastering these fundamental stages is prerequisite for reliable time-series reasoning and remains an immediate challenge. Recent works have shown that Vision Language Models (VLMs) can excel in basic TSR tasks by relying exclusively on time series visualizations (Zhang et al., 2025a; Sen et al., 2025). However, such vision-centric approaches frequently struggle with numerical reasoning tasks that require precise value-based evidence. Inspired by (Wei et al., 2025), which utilizes visual tokens to represent textual information efficiently, we introduce robust VLM-based TSRM named Large Language and Time Series Assistant (LLATISA). LLATISA is designed to bridge the gap between qualitative visual intuition and quantitative numerical precision. By employing three-stage curriculum finetuning strategy aligned with our proposed L1-L3 hierarchy, LLATISA demonstrates that TSR benefits from incremental cognitive grounding and highfidelity Chains-of-Thought (CoT). In summary, our contributions are summarized as follows: cognitive taxonomy and unified dataset. We formalize four-level cognitive taxonomy (L11We analyze these findings in Sections A.1 and D. L4) that captures the progressive nature of temporal understanding. Based on this, we introduce HITSR, large-scale, high-fidelity TSR dataset of over 83k samples spanning L1-L3, featuring diverse task settings and verified reasoning chains. numerically-enhanced TSRM. We propose LLATISA, VLM-based TSRM that bridges qualitative perception and quantitative precision. LLATISA is trained via three-stage curriculum aligned with our L1-L3 hierarchy to build robust reasoning capabilities progressively. Empirical validation of multi-stage reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LLATISA consistently outperforms proprietary models and various open-source baselines. Furthermore, our model exhibits superior generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios across existing benchmarks.

sections.title: 2 Related Works
sections.content: 2.1 Time Series Reasoning Datasets Recently released multimodal time series datasets adopt various types of TSR tasks. For general question-answering, early efforts like TSQA (Kong et al., 2025) and TRQA (Jing et al., 2025) explore LLMs potential in question-answering and traditional time series analysis tasks. SciTS (Wu et al., 2025) introduces benchmark specifically designed for scientific time series analysis across wide range of scientific domains. RATs40k (Yang et al., 2025b) transforms classical time series anomaly detection from discriminative into reasoning-intensive task and annotates samples with explanatory reasoning. TimeMMD (Liu et al., 2024a)is tailored for the context-aware time series forecasting task. MMTS-Bench (Yin et al., 2026) provides multi-dimensional task classification taxonomy, yet it overlooks evaluating models basic numerical read-out and combining diverse semantics of real scenarios. In addition to insufficient context, current benchmarks are further hampered by semantic ambiguities in label definitions and lowfidelity question-answer pairs (Guan et al., 2025). Moreover, most of the datasets focus on either pattern description or complex reasoning scenarios such as forecasting, and overlook evaluating models basic ability of read-out numerical values. Our findings suggest that current models even struggle with finding point-level numerical evidence, suffering from overthinking or hallucinations with longer time horizons. 2.2 Time Series Reasoning Models Understanding time series via language models generally mostly follows three encoding paradigms: (i) direct serialization into text tokens, (ii) visual rendering as plots for VLMs, and (iii) the integration of dedicated time-series encoders to construct Time-Series Multimodal Large Language Models (TS-MLLMs). TimeOmni-1 (Guan et al., 2025) and Time-R1 (Luo et al., 2025) employ textual inputs for context-aware forecasting. ChatTS (Xie et al., 2025b) and ITFormer (Wang et al., 2025b) incorporate an MLP-based and Q-former (Li et al., 2023) style time series encoder to perform multivariate TSR tasks, respectively. TimeMaster (Zhang et al., 2025a) enables VLMs to perform interpretable classification based on visualized time series. While recent studies point out that TSRMs struggle with basic TSR tasks like description differentiation (Sen et al., 2025), VLMs have demonstrated superior robustness compared to their LLM and TS-MLLM counterparts. This observation motivates us to further unlock the potential of VLMs for general-purpose TSR scenarios.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: 3.1 Time Series Reasoning Taxonomy We argue that reliable TSR is inherently compositional, necessitating hierarchy that can: (i) faithfully ground observations to timestamps, (ii) abstract salient temporal patterns beyond point-level evidence, and (iii) integrate with contextual knowledge to support semantic judgment and prediction. In contrast, prior TSR benchmarks are typically organized around isolated tasks and adopt heterogeneous definitions of what constitutes reasoning, often over-emphasizing narrow subset of problem types (e.g., coarse pattern discrimination). Such fragmentation leads to two limitations. First, cross-benchmark comparability is compromised because mismatched capability sets and inconsistent task difficulties prevent unified assessment of progress. Second, current TSRMs often attempt to perform high-level reasoning (e.g., forecasting) on an unsteady foundation: models still struggle with elementary grounding and verification, which undermines the credibility and interpretability of more complex reasoning. To address these limitations, we introduce difficulty-stratified taxonomy that organizes TSR into progressively increasing levels of complexity. This framework serves as unified schema for categorizing diverse TSR tasks and provides diagnostic lens to pinpoint the specific cognitive boundaries of current models. Concretely, we decompose TSR into four hierarchical levels: L1: Numerical Read-out. Establish time-aware indexing and point-level numerical retrieval. L2: Pattern Perception. Identify and differentiate multi-scale temporal patterns using quantitative evidence. L3: Semantic Reasoning. Integrate time series observations with contextual knowledge to perform domain-specific reasoning. L4: Predictive Inference. Generate high-fidelity time-series predictions. Based on such taxonomy, we summarize current benchmarks into different levels in Section A.2, posing general standard to task definition and evaluation of TSRMs capabilities. 3.2 HITSR Dataset Building on our four-level TSR taxonomy, we introduce HITSR, unified dataset for both training and evaluation of TSR capabilities across levels L1-L3. Unlike prior benchmarks that may contain ambiguous queries or underspecified context, HITSR dataset is constructed with rigorous validity checks to ensure exact problem statements and Figure 2: Overview of data pipeline and the model framework. (a) Constructions of HITSR, including data collection, sample annotation, and multi-stage verification. (b) Framework of LLATISA, which incorporates dual-image inputs and performed (c) three-stage curriculum fine-tuning on the HITSR. sufficient contextual information. Overall, HITSR comprises approximately 83k samples spanning synthetic and real-world time series, annotated and verified via multi-stage process. In total, Data Sources. the HITSR dataset comprises three hierarchical levels: HITSR-L1 (30,000 samples), HITSR-L2 (50,703 samples), and HITSR-L3 (3,121 samples). Each subset includes high-fidelity test set rigorously verified by human annotators. For HITSR-L1 and HITSRL2, our primary objective is to provide large-scale supervision that strengthens fundamental reasoning abilities in controlled yet diverse manner. We therefore generate HITSR-L1 and HITSR-L2 instances using synthetic time series sourced from (Xie et al., 2025b). This approach allows us to systematically manipulate temporal structures and underlying patterns, while scaling the data to accommodate various training-budget requirements without being restricted by the scarcity of real-world annotations. For HITSR-L3, we curate publicly available real-world time series from diverse domains and construct question-answer pairs written by the LLM analyzer. The complete data synthesis pipeline and the summary of real-world data information are detailed in Section B. Task Formulation. Following the proposed taxonomy, we instantiate set of subtasks within each level to cover complementary facets of the TSR as shown in Section C.1. L1 tasks are formulated as short-answer questions, given that the ground truth can be generated via rule-based methods. Tasks within L2-L3 are structured as multiple-choice (MC) questions to ensure objective and reproducible evaluation. To maintain strict instructionfollowing orientation and minimize biases from formatting artifacts, each supervision target is crafted as complete natural-language statement rather than concise label. Detailed task definitions and additional examples are provided in Section C. Additionally, we adopt different answer-construction protocols tailored to the degree of output determinacy. For L1 and the Numerical Perception subtask in L2, the correct option can be derived by explicit rules from the underlying series; we therefore generate options programmatically under fixed templates. For the two Pattern Differentiation subtasks in L2 and the L3 Semantic Understanding task, the outputs are descriptive and require higher-level interpretation. We construct these items with an LLM-assisted pipeline and subsequently verify by humans to ensure correctness and remove ambiguous cases. Multi-Stage Verified CoT Annotation. We adopt distinct annotation and verification pipelines for L2 and L3 tasks. For L2 Pattern Differentiation, we first prompt GPT-5 with each time series visual plot and key statistics (e.g., extreme valueindex pairs, spike locations with precise values, and notable local events) to produce numerically aware descriptions of local and global patterns. We then construct multiple-choice questions by sampling distractor options from descriptions of other series. To ensure reliability, we employ GPT-5 to cross-validate each question, removing ambiguous descriptions and flawed items (e.g., those with non-unique correct options). Finally, we hold out 10% of the validated pool for evaluation. This subset undergoes comprehensive human auditing, and only items that pass this rigorous review are retained, yielding unambiguous test set. For L3 Semantic Understanding, we leverage LLMs to generate scenario-aware multiple-choice questions by prompting them with real-world time series and their associated metadata. Each scenario description integrates pattern recognition and numerical grounding, effectively encapsulating the TSRMs capabilities across L1-L3. Each item is subsequently dual-checked by GPT-5 and then reviewed by humans to comprehensively verify that the correct answer is supported by consistent numerical, pattern, and semantic evidence. 3.3 LLATISA Time series can be presented to language models in various formats (Sen et al., 2025). While purely visual plots effectively convey global shapes and local morphological cues, they often lack the finegrained numerical precision required for grounded reasoning. Consequently, many TSR methods typically represent time series as textualized numerical sequences (Guan et al., 2025; Kong et al., 2025) or integrate LLMs with dedicated time series encoders to develop TS-MLLMs (Xie et al., 2025b; Zhang et al., 2025b). Recent studies, however, indicate that TS-MLLMs frequently underperform relative to general-purpose LLMs or VLMs on L2 TSR tasks, while VLMs are stronger on pattern-centric tasks (e.g., local pattern differentiation) (Sen et al., 2025), underscoring the advantages of visual representations for L2 reasoning. To empower VLMs with precise numerical grounding, we propose LLATISA (Figure 2.b), dual-view input framework that pairs standard time series visualizations with secondary image rendering the data as structured index-value table. This design facilitates progressive interpretation paradigm: the first plot enables macroscopic inspection for overall perception, the image of the structured table enables point-accurate verification of specific indices or intervals. By synthesizing these dual modalities alongside textual context, the model produces semantically grounded reasoning. Empirically, compared to processing series as textual arrays, LLATISA provides extra index information, thereby significantly mitigating numerical hallucinations and improving performance on numerical-sensitive tasks. Leveraging HITSR, LLATISA is trained via three-stage curriculum designed to progressively acquire the prerequisite capabilities defined in our taxonomy. During the initial two stages, we perform sequential Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on HITSR-L1 and HITSR-L2 to consolidate the models numerical read-out precision and patIn the third stage, tern perception capabilities. LLATISA could be either trained on HITSR-L3 or general-purpose domain-specific TSR benchmarks. Our experiments further show that existing TSRMs often fail even on basic L1-L2 TSR, and struggle to combine LLM priors with series-level evidence for reliable L3 semantic inference. In contrast, curriculum-trained LLATISA across L1-L2 could be easily transferred to domain-specific L3 tasks (e.g., ECG interpretation).

sections.title: 4 Experiment
sections.content: Our experiments focus on investigating the following research questions (RQs): RQ1: How does LLATISA perform in existing TSR benchmarks across levels L1-L3? RQ2: How does LLATISA perform compared with different encoding strategies? RQ3: How does LLATISA perform in generalizing to real-world applications? RQ4: How does the curriculum tuning strategy and CoT-aware training data benefit LLATISAs performance? 4.1 Experiment Settings To leverage the VLMs fundamental capability in interpreting numeric-centric images, LLATISA employs Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025) as its backbone. To ensure fair comparison, we report results exclusively on out-of-distribution (OOD) datasets across levels L1-L3 (see Table 1). To demonstrate that LLATISA serves as strong foundation model when fine-tuned for new domain, we further evaluate it on ECG interpretation under both in-distribution (ID) and OOD settings using the ECG-Grounding dataset (Lan et al., 2025), as shown in Table 3. Comprehensive training specifications, including parameter configurations and the proportions of training datasets across levels L1-L3, are also provided in Section E. Baselines. We conduct extensive experiments across various time series encoding strategies, including: (i) encoding series as textual arrays; (ii) visualizing as sequence plots or numerical grids (denoted as plot and num in our tables); (iii) adopting dual-modality input comprising both visualizations and textual sequences; and (iv) leveraging TS-MLLMs. Since LLATISA incorporates index information in the second image, we also construct two types of textual encoding methods, w/ index and w/o index, to ensure fair comparison. For closed-source models, we select GPT-4o (Achiam et al., 2023), utilizing it as both LLMs and VLMs. For open-source models, we employ Qwen3-8B (Yang et al., 2025a) and its multimodal counterpart, Qwen3-VL-8B (Bai et al., 2025), to evaluate the efficacy of our curriculum fine-tuning strategy and to conduct comparative analysis of various training paradigms. We also incorporate textual-encoding TSRMs, such as Time-R1 (Luo et al., 2025) and Time-MQA (Kong et al., 2025). For TS-MLLMs, we evaluate ChatTS (Xie et al., 2025b) alongside OpenTSLM (Langer et al., 2025). For ECG interpretation, we include GEM (Lan et al., 2025) and PULSE (Liu et al., 2024b) as domain-specific methods. We adopt standardized instruction formats for different time series encoding strategies to ensure fair comparison. Detailed information on baseline models and corresponding instruction templates is provided in Section F. Datasets. For the L1 extreme value localization task, we construct OOD evaluations using the realworld subset of HITSR-L1, given that LLATISA is trained exclusively on its synthetic counterpart. Regarding L2, we employ BEDTime (Sen et al., 2025) and MMTS-Bench, which focus on local and global pattern differentiation, respectively. To balance inference overhead, we randomly sample 500 questions from BEDTime for testing, while the complete corresponding subset of MMTS-Bench (120 samples) is utilized. For L3, we use MCQ2 (Merrill et al., 2024) to assess multi-series contextual reasoning. Following the experimental setting of (Xie et al., 2025b), we randomly sample 100 questions from MCQ2. To further verify LLATISAs potential as robust foundation model for L3-level real-world multivariate TSR, we conduct domain-specific fine-tuning on ECGGrounding (Lan et al., 2025), assessing the models transferability across both ID and OOD scenarios. We report accuracy (Acc) as the primary metric for evaluation across all tasks. Additionally, we report the success rate (SR) to assess the models ability to generate valid answers. 4.2 OOD Experiment Results As shown in Table 1, we conduct comprehensive OOD evaluations comparing LLATISA with various baselines across the L1-L3 levels. The results demonstrate that LLATISA achieves significant improvements across diverse TSR scenarios. Specifically, in the L1 point-level value localization task, we observe markedly lower success rate on textbased models. These models frequently struggle to output valid answers, often becoming trapped in repetitive generation loops that lead to sequences exceeding the maximum context length. 4.3 Impact of TS Representation Strategies To empirically validate the efficacy and efficiency of LLATISAs dual-view encoding strategy, we perform comprehensive comparison against various time-series representation strategies (introduced in Section 4.1) under the same backbone and training configuration, and evaluate performance on OOD benchmarks at levels L1-L3. Performance Comparison. Table 2 presents the comprehensive performance on OOD testbeds across various time series representation strategies. LLATISA consistently outperforms alternative strategies on the majority of OOD tasks across levels L1-L3, demonstrating its superior efficacy in TSR. Under the curriculum learning strategy, almost all strategies show performance gains over their untuned counterparts. For textbased paradigms (notably the w/o index variant), the success rate for L1 tasks improves markedly with fine-tuning. Furthermore, incorporating explicit index information significantly improves the precision of point-level localization, leading to substantial improvements in both accuracy and SR. 4.4 Application in ECG Interpretation To evaluate the generalization capabilities of LLATISA (previously fine-tuned on HITSR), we further perform Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on the ECG-Grounding 30k dataset. For rigorous comparison, we also fine-tune Qwen3-VL-8B on the same dataset. Baseline results for ECG-specific models are directly sourced from (Lan et al., 2025). The full results are shown in Table 17. As illustrated in Table 3, LLATISA exhibits distinct advantage in lead-wise evaluation, which directly reflects its adherence to the structured, 12-lead diagnostic procedure employed by professional clinicians. Specifically, LLATISA achieves remarkable gains in lead assessment coverage and accuracy, outperforming GEM (LLaVA) by 18.14% and 14.22% in the ID evaluation, respectively. These results indicate that LLATISA more consistently executes per-lead analysis and generates more reliable lead-specific statements. Compared to Qwen3-VL-8B, LLATISA demonstrates superior performance across all metrics, with notable improvements on diagnostic accuracy (5.42%) and evidence-based reasoning (4.39%) in the OOD scenario. This high data efficiency demonstrates that LLATISA can be efficiently specialized for grounded ECG interpretation and highlights its potential as general TSR foundation model for realworld applications. 4.5 Ablation Study Effectiveness of CoT. In the HITSR dataset, we incorporate LLM-annotated CoT data, verified by humans, to enhance LLATISAs performance and generalization, particularly on the more complex tasks at levels L2-L3. To isolate the impact of CoT, we perform SFT on the same dataset using only the correct options as supervision (w/o CoT). As shown in Table 4, while ID performance does not degrade significantly, the OOD performance deteriorates markedly, with accuracy decreasing by 4.31% and 17.91% on L2 and L3, respectively. Furthermore, training solely on correct options significantly undermines the models original instructionfollowing capabilities. We observe that the model tuned without CoT data fails to generate reasoning chains, even when explicitly prompted to do so. This phenomenon highlights the critical role of HITSR as thinking-aware TSR training resource. By integrating high-quality CoT trajectories, our benchmark not only incentivizes robust cross-task reasoning but also synergistically preserves the foundational generative alignment of the backbone model. As illustrated in Table 4, single-stage joint training consistently degrades performance, yielding drops of 2.06% and 3.45% in ID tasks for L2 and L3, respectively, and reductions of 1.74% and 14.93% under OOD evaluation for L2 and L3. While the joint-training model maintains relatively stable performance on ID scenarios and simpler OOD tasks (L2), it exhibits significant decline on OOD L3 tasks, suggesting that without curriculum learning, the model struggles to internalize the logic needed for complex semantic reasoning, thereby limiting generalization.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we propose difficulty-stratified view of TSR and formalize four-level taxonomy (L1L4) that characterizes progressive TSR capabilities. We introduce HITSR, large-scale hierarchical dataset with over 83k high-fidelity TSR questions spanning L1-L3, constructed with unambiguous labels and verifiable reasoning chains to support both reliable training and evaluation. Building on HITSR, we present LLATISA, VLM-based TSR model that pairs time series plots with an auxiliary index-value table to bridge qualitative perception and precise numerical evidence, and is trained via three-stage curriculum aligned with L1-L3. Extensive experiments on diverse OOD benchmarks and real-world scenarios show that LLATISA consistently outperforms strong baselines and different modeling strategies, and transfers effectively to domain-specific semantic reasoning, suggesting practical path toward more robust unified TSRMs.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Our study primarily focuses on systematic dataset construction and supervised curriculum fine-tuning, leaving the exploration of Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning (RFT) on HITSR as future direction. Effectively implementing RFT in this domain remains challenging due to the complexity of reward design, which must simultaneously supervise low-level numerical precision and high-level semantic logic across our L1-L4 taxonomy. Future work includes: (i) integrating RL into the hierarchical curriculum to further refine the models policy; (ii) investigating robust initialization strategies to mitigate cold-start issues.

sections.title: Ethics Statement
sections.content: All datasets and language models used in this work are publicly available. The HITSR dataset was constructed from well-established, publicly accessible time series benchmarks and synthetic data generation pipelines, and LLATISA is built on the open-sourced model.

sections.title: A Findings
sections.content: A.1 Model Analysis Our research is primarily motivated by critical appraisal of existing TSR benchmarks. Preliminary evaluations of GPT-4o on local pattern differentiation tasks reveal significant deficiencies in handling foundational TSR operations, echoing the observations in (Sen et al., 2025). As illustrated in Table 18, GPT-4o fails to perform accurate numerical grounding when interpreting visual plot; it struggles to discern the precise relationship between start and end points or characterize the specific rate of change (e.g., confusing abrupt drops with "smooth" decreases). This diagnostic case underscores pivotal prerequisite: establishing robust numerical read-out capabilities for TSRMs is essential before tackling the higher-order reasoning required in L3 and L4 tasks. Furthermore, our empirical results across both ID  (Table 15)  and OOD  (Table 1)  scenarios demonstrate that current LLMs, VLMs, and even specialized TSRMs fall short in A.2 Dataset Analysis We systematically categorize existing TSR datasets according to our proposed four-level taxonomy in Table 16, through which we identify several critical deficiencies in current benchmarks. For instance, certain questions exhibit label ambiguity, containing multiple valid options (Tables 20 and 21), while others lack sufficient contextual information for logical reasoning  (Table 22)  . Furthermore, most TSR datasets are designed exclusively for evaluation rather than providing comprehensive trainingtest splits. Among the limited training data available, the absence of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotations is major drawback, while solely relying on correct labels for training tends to degrade the generative reasoning capabilities of TSRMs.

sections.title: B Data Source
sections.content: B.1 Synthetic Time Series We use the complete time series synthesis pipeline introduced by (Xie et al., 2025b). To avoid ambiguity in options and control question quality, we merge some of the preset attributions to ensure that the remaining attributes do not introduce multiple correct options when used to create local pattern recognition problems. The new attribution pool is summarized in Table 5. B.2 Real-world Time Series We utilize real-world time series data for: (i) creating OOD test sets for the L1 task; (ii) constructing semantic understanding subsets. For the L1 evaluations reported in Table 1, we select datasets as follows: ETT (4 subsets) (Zhou et al., 2021) contains group of four subsets of oil temperature and power load collected by electricity transformers from July 2016 to July 2018, with minutes or hourly recorded frequency. Weather (Wetterstation, 2020) records meteorological time series with 21 weather indicators collected every 10 minutes from the Weather Station of the Max Planck Biogeochemistry Institute in 2020. Exchange Rate (Lai et al., 2018) includes the daily exchange rates of eight different countries, ranging from 1990 to 2016. Type attribution Trend Global Seasonality Frequency Noise Spike Turning point Local Caption increase, decrease, steady, multi-stage; sin, square, triangle, none; high, low; noisy, clean; upward, downward; rises then falls, falls then rises; Min/Max [index, value] pairs; Numerical Start/End values; Key event positions spike, turning point; Table 5: Overview of synthetic time series attributions. Traffic (Wu et al., 2021) encompasses the hourly measures of road occupancy rates obtained from 862 sensors situated in the San Francisco Bay area freeways between January 2015 and December 2016. Electricity (Wu et al., 2021) mainly consists of hourly electricity consumption data from 321 customers. To facilitate the semantic understanding task, we augment our dataset with large-scale time series repositories, including UTSD (Liu et al., 2024c), Monash (Godahewa et al., 2021), and Time-MMD (Liu et al., 2024a). These datasets provide rich meta-annotations that serve as the basis for generating ground-truth options. Each time series is subjected to random cropping to generate sub-series, thereby expanding the data volume and enhancing descriptive diversity. Comprehensive statistics of these datasets are provided in Table 13. For each sample, we prompt GPT-5 with the raw time series (represented as textual arrays) and its associated metadata to synthesize scenario-aligned MCQs. The ground truth is derived from the actual data source, while distractors are engineered to be erroneous in terms of numerical precision, temporal pattern perception, or contextual consistency, which correspond to levels L1L3 in our TSR taxonomy. We further define two semantic evaluation settings: (i) single-scenario, where all options describe the series within the same metadata context, and (ii) multi-scenario, where the LLM is instructed to generate cross-contextual wrong options that deviate from the ground truth.

sections.title: C Details of HITSR
sections.content: C.1 Task definition The full task definition is summarized in Table 14. C.2 Data Verification L1: Numerical Read-out. The question-answer (QA) pairs utilized in this stage are completely constructed by strict rule-based annotations based on each time series attribution pool. L2: Pattern Perception. For numerical perception, it is similar to L1, which generated the complete QA pairs through an explicit rule-based pipeline. The distractors are created by slightly perturbing the numerical evidence. For local pattern differentiation, distractors are synthesized using rule-based perturbations, such as numerical manipulation or random attribute sampling from heterogeneous series. To ensure label uniqueness, we perform cross-verification by benchmarking each candidate option against the ground-truth attributes of the series. Subsequently, we use GPT-5 with both the metadata and visual plots of the given time series to annotate the correct answers with CoT reasoning. For quality assurance, the training set only includes samples correctly resolved by GPT-5, while the test set undergoes rigorous human verification to ensure high fidelity. Regarding global pattern differentiation, we first prompt GPT-5 to generate comprehensive descriptions of the series. Distractors are then constructed by incorporating annotations from disparate sequences. Similarly, the original dataset is processed by GPT-5 to produce reasoning-enhanced ground truth, ensuring the depth and accuracy of the labels. L3: Semantic Understanding. The raw QA pairs are initially synthesized by GPT-5 in single pass. To ensure data integrity, we implement cross-validation stage where GPT-5 evaluates the generated pairs against set of rigorous criteria. This evaluation focuses on two critical dimensions: (i) verifying the ground truth, ensuring that numerical evidence, pattern characterization, and semantic logic are technically sound; and (ii) auditing distractors, where each incorrect option is reexamined to eliminate potential overlaps or counterexamples that might inadvertently align with the time series. Finally, manual dual-check is performed on the validated candidates to finalize high-fidelity, real-world TSR dataset. C.3 Prompt Templates The prompt templates used during the construction of HITSR are summarized as follows: L2: Annotation: Figure 3. L2: CoT Generation: Figure 4. L3: QA Generation: Figure 5. L3: Question Examination: Figure 6. C.4 Illustration of HITSR We provide QA sample for each subtask within HITSR, which is summarized as follows: Numerical Read-out: Tables 23 to 26. Pattern Perception: Tables 27 to 29. Semantic Reasoning: Table 30. C.5 Comparison to Existed TSR Datesets The differences among HITSR and existing TSR datasets are summarized as follows: The Enrichment of Verified Chain-of-Thought. Different from evaluation-only benchmarks (e.g., BEDTime (Sen et al., 2025), TimeMCQ2 (Merrill et al., 2024), and MMTS-Bench (Yin et al., 2026)), HITSR provides 83k samples enriched with verified Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories. By transforming raw data into high-quality instruction-tuning pairs, we enable TSRMs to learn the "process of reasoning" rather than solely mapping correct answers. Our results (Section 4.5) show that this approach significantly enhances the LLATISAs performance in complex reasoning tasks while preserving the models general instruction-following capabilities. Unambiguous and Logic-Grounded CoT. In contrast to the datasets with noisy (see Table 13-14 in the manuscript) or purely semantic annotations, our CoT follows strict Perceptionto-Reasoning logic (Numerical -> Pattern -> Semantic). Each step is cross-verified against ground-truth data, ensuring that the reasoning path is grounded in numerical reality rather than textual hallucination. Emphasis on Foundational TSR Capabilities. Most existing studies (e.g., Time-MMD (Liu et al., 2024a), TSR-Suite (Guan et al., 2025)) focus on L2-L4 high-level TSR tasks but overlook the verification of models numerical readout ability, especially in long-horizon settings. HITSR uniquely introduces L1 tasks (precise grounding and localization) as diagnostic anchor. This ensures that complex TSR abilities are evaluated only after verifying the models foundational perceptual accuracy. clear difficulty-Stratified taxonomy for TSR. Guided by our 4-level taxonomy (grounded in Blooms Taxonomy (Bloom et al., 1956) and Bertins Levels of Reading (Monmonier, 1985)), HITSR acts as systematic diagnostic framework. Unlike fragmented benchmarks, HITSRs stratified design allows for curriculum learning (L1 L3) and enables researchers to pinpoint exactly where TSRMs reasoning chain breaks down.

sections.title: D Evaluation on the HITSR
sections.content: We evaluate representative baseline models on the HITSR test set, comprising 100 randomly sampled questions, with results summarized in Table 15. Key observations are as follows: First, incorporating temporal indices into textualized numerical arrays significantly enhances the reasoning performance of LLMs; for instance, GPT-4os L1 accuracy improves from 6.0% to 23.0% with indices, particularly aiding numerical grounding tasks. Second, ChatTS demonstrates strong capability to estimate approximate values while being poor at mapping the correct index for the target value, achieving competitive 70.0% in semantic understanding (L3), which is merely 1.0% performance gap compared to the best-performing multimodal GPT-4o. consistent performance disparity exists between local and global pattern differentiation, revealing the limitations of single-modality reasoning. While pure vision or text-only models excel in global perception (e.g., GPT-4o reaching 93.0% and 94.0% in L2-Global, respectively), they both struggle with local granularity, as evidenced by their stagnant performance in L2-Local (hovering around 55.0%). This bottleneck is exemplified in Table 18, where pure vision fails to discern precise temporal transitions, such as confusing sharp step-drop with smooth decrease. Crucially, our results demonstrate significant performance leap when Vision and Text modalities are integrated, with GPT-4os L2-Local accuracy rising to 69.0%. This synergistic gain highlights the effectiveness of dual-stream input paradigmutilizing visual plots for overall pattern recognition and textual sequences for numerical precision. These findings suggest that future TSRMs should prioritize the fusion of visual global context and numerical fine-grained features to overcome the inherent perceptual gaps of isolated modalities. For the Numerical Perception task in L2, we introduce subtle numerical perturbations into local pattern differentiation to synthesize challenging distractors. Experimental results in Table 15 show that Qwen3-VL-8B achieves the state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance of 78.0% when equipped with both vision and indexed textual series inputs. Besides, we observe significant performance degradation for Qwen3-8B in the "Text (w/o index)" setting (36.0%); this is primarily due to its inefficient regressive value retrieval patterns, which often exceed the maximum output length, thus fail to answer the question. Furthermore, the Visiononly modality consistently yields the lower scores across models (e.g., 58.0% for GPT-4o and 47.0% for Qwen3-VL). Such failure of visual heuristics underscores the success of our dataset construction: the Numerical Perception task necessitates reliance on precise numerical evidence rather than coarse visual patterns, thereby establishing more rigorous benchmark for fine-grained TSR. two types of architectures. It employs curriculum training paradigm, which first trains on TS-QA datasets and then transfers to fine-tune on medicalspecific TSR tasks. F. sections.title: F Baseline Methods
sections.content: F.1 Time Series Reasoning Models Time-MQA (Kong et al., 2025). Time-MQA is an LLM-based TSRM that employs LoRA to finetune different backbone models on large-scale TSQA dataset that contains traditional time series analysis and QA tasks. sections.title: H AI Assistants Usage
sections.content: In this paper, the AI Assistants are only used for text polishing. Theoretical Underpinning of the Difficulty-Stratified Taxonomy Our four-level decomposition of TSR is not arbitrary but is grounded in two well-established theoretical frameworks: Blooms Taxonomy (Bloom et al., 1956) (cognitive psychology) and Bertins Levels of Reading (Monmonier, 1985) (visual analytic). Cognitive Reasoning (Blooms Taxonomy): Our hierarchy mirrors the progression from LowOrder Thinking Skills (LOTS) to High-Order Thinking Skills (HOTS). Specifically: (i) L1 (Numerical Read-out) maps to Remembering/Retrieving (discrete fact extraction); (ii) L2 (Pattern Perception) maps to Understanding (identifying structural relationships and trends); (iii) L3 (Semantic Reasoning) maps to Applying/Analyzing (contextualizing patterns within domain-specific knowledge); and (iv) L4 (Predictive Inference) maps to Creating (generating future states or logical relationships based on historical temporal data). Recent studies (Huber and Niklaus, 2025) have formally advocated for using Blooms Taxonomy to evaluate the reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Visual Perception (Bertins Levels of Reading): From data visualization perspective, Jacques Bertins classic framework justifies our structural necessity: (i) Elementary Level (L1) focuses on single data element; (ii) Intermediate/Global Level (L2) focuses on relationships and macroscopic trends across multiple elements; and (iii) Extrapolative Level (L3/L4) involves deducing hidden semantics or future trends beyond the given data. By aligning with these frameworks, HITSR provides principled road-map to evaluate whether TSRMs failure stems from perceptual bottleneck (L1-L2) or reasoning deficit (L3-L4). This structured decomposition is necessary for diagnosing the precise cognitive boundaries of TSRMs.

sections.title: J Position of Our Work
sections.content: We emphasize that our paper intentionally focuses on TSR-Understanding (L1-L3) rather than TSRGeneration (L4). We argue that these are two fundamentally different paradigms. Paradigm Distinction. In the broader multimodal field, understanding models (e.g., Qwen3VL) and generative model (e.g., the diffusion model) are typically treated as distinct research directions. Integrating L4 (especially numerical forecasting) would necessitate transition from Reasoning Model to Unified Understandingand-Generation Model, which presents distinct technical challenges (e.g., specialized generative losses) and constitutes separate research frontier. Diagnostic Purity. Our primary goal is to provide diagnostic lens to pinpoint the cognitive boundaries of current TSRMs. By focusing on L1-L3, we ensure rigorous evaluation of the models analytical capabilities without the confounding factors of generative precision. Evaluation on L4 Task We have conducted evaluations on the L4 (Predictive Inference) task and incorporated L4 into Modality Model Acc% Text (w/o index) GPT-4.1 GPT-4o Claude-3.5-Sonnet Qwen3-8B LLaMA3.1-8B Vision (plot + num) GPT-4o Qwen3-VL-8B LLATISA (L1L2L3) LLATISA (L1L2L3L4) 79.1 75.6 82.2 67.1 41.0 78.3 42.1 54.2 83.3 Table 6: distribution datasets in L4 (Generative Inference). Performance comparison on out-ofHITSR. We then trained LLATISA on this subset and evaluated its out-of-distribution (OOD) performance on real-world time series data. K.1 Task Introduction Given time series segment x, the model is tasked with Multiple Choice (MC) question to identify the unique chronological successor from pool of four candidate patches y1, y2, y3, y4. While the correct successor follows immediately in the original series, the negative candidates are sampled from distinct time series with diverse characteristics. This setup evaluates the models ability to extrapolate underlying temporal dynamics and distinguish the true evolution of series from statistically distinct alternatives. K.2 Dataset Construction We extended our synthetic generation pipeline to ensure that L4 tasks are both challenging and unambiguous. To prevent shared ground truth (where multiple options could be plausible), we implemented multi-stage filtering protocol for negative candidates: Statistical Uniqueness. We filter out candidates with high Pearson Correlation ( 0.8) or small Euclidean Distance to the ground truth to ensure distinctiveness. Strategic Truncation. To avoid introducing excessive ambiguity, we ensure the split point between and does not occur during highvariance events, such as mid-spike or at sharp turning points. Splitting at such critical junctures may cause multiple plausible answers; by selecting structurally stable partition points, we ensure the ground truth is the only logically consistent successor. Acc% 16.9 23.0 43.0 21.4 36.8 75.4 32.8 54.2 Trend Smoothness. We use weighted continuation score, which incorporates gradient consistency and tail-to-head difference to verify that the positive sample is the most semantically and numerically plausible successor.

sections.title: Ablation
sections.content: only L1 only L2 only L3 Mutual Exclusion. Negative candidates are cross-checked to ensure low mutual correlation, preventing redundant options within single question. K.3 Transition to Vision Modalities To align with the dual-view strategy of LLATISA, we transform the L4 predictive task into dualimage input format. Specifically, the visualized patterns are rendered as 2 2 grid of subplots, each representing one of the four options. Within each subplot, the lookback context and the candidate prediction patch are visualized in blue and red, respectively, allowing the model to perform visual comparative analysis of temporal continuity. Complementing this, precision-calibrated numerical table provides exact values for the lookback window. This dual-view setup ensures the model can leverage global trend intuition from the plots while maintaining the fine-grained numerical grounding necessary for rigorous successor identification. K.4 Experiment Results Following this rigorous protocol, we expanded our curriculum to include fourth stage (L1L2L3L4). We trained LLATISA on 4,993 L4-specific questions for 2 epochs (learning rate 1 105) and evaluated its OOD performance on the real-world TRQA (Jing et al., 2025) subset. As results shown in table 6, we summarize key observations as follows: Surpassing Text-only TSRMs. LLATISA achieves superior performance compared to both closef-source and open-source TSRMs with textual time series input settings. These results further underscore the effectiveness of LLATISAs dual-view multimodal paradigm, high-quality TSR dataset (HITSR), and multi-stage curriculum fine-tuning. Comparison with General VLMs. LLATISA significantly outperforms its backbone model Qwen3-VL-8B. This discrepancy highlights the necessity of our dual-view representation and multi-stage training on the difficulty-stratified TSR datasets. L1+L2 L1+L2+L3 L1+L2+L3+L4 L1L2 L1L2L3 LLATISA (L1L2L3L4) 83.3 Table 7: Ablation study on the L4 task. All experiments are conducted on OOD datasets. Efficacy of the L4 Curriculum Stage. The transition from the L3-trained model (54.2%) to the L4-trained model (83.3%) reveals substantial 29.1% improvement. This confirms that while foundational understanding (L1-L3) provides necessary base, specific predictive inference training (L4) is essential for generalizing the TSR capability from understanding to the generative tasks. Robustness in OOD Scenarios. Since the evaluation was conducted on real-world data from TRQA (which differs significantly from our synthetic training distributions), the high accuracy (83.3%) serves as strong evidence of LLATISAs cross-domain generalization. The model has learned the underlying principles of temporal continuity rather than memorizing specific patterns. Overall, these results provide definitive answer to the reviewers concerns: LLATISA is not only diagnostic tool for understanding but also has the potential to generalize to L4 predictive tasks. K.5 Ablation Study To further investigate the synergy between our difficulty-stratified taxonomy and the curriculum training regime, we conducted additional ablation studies on the L4 task. We compared LLATISA against single-stage training and joint multi-task learning to verify if foundational levels (L1L3) are prerequisites for predictive inference (L4). Here, + refers to joint training on shuffled datasets from multiple levels (e.g., L1+L2), while denotes sequential curriculum regime (e.g., L1L2). Evaluation results  (table 7)  underscore the critical importance of our sequential curriculum: single-stage training fails on L4 tasks without the foundational grounding provided by L1L3. Notably, LLaTiSA outperforms joint training (L1+L2+L3+L4) by 7.9%, confirming that step-by-step mastery of temporal primitives and semantics effectively improves the performance of complex predictive inference. This validates that our difficulty-stratified taxonomy acts as necessary cognitive scaffolding, ensuring robust generalization in complex OOD scenarios.

sections.title: L Expansion of Experiments
sections.content: L.1 Ablation Study To justify the necessity of the multi-stage SFT, we provide intermediate evaluations of LLATISA across different training stages on both ID and OOD benchmarks (Table below). Here, L1 + L2 refers to joint training on shuffled datasets from L1-L2, while L1 L2 denotes two-stage curriculum fine-tuning regime. The evaluation results  (table 10)  reveal three critical insights that justify the necessity of our three-stage curriculum: As shown in the Only L2 row, although the model masters pattern perception, its L1 performance significantly collapses. This proves that high-level TSR does not naturally "backwards inherit" foundational perception. The L1 stage is essential to anchor the TSRMs grounding capability. While training on L2 alone provides some reasoning capability, the performance on L3 improves when L1 and L2 are combined (from 66.0% to 70.0% ID). The full curriculum (LLATISA) achieves the highest L3 score (87.0% ID, 67.0% OOD). LLATISA consistently outperforms L1 L2 and L1 + L2 strategies, indicating the effectiveness of the training on the L3 part and multi-stage curriculum regime, respectively. In conclusion, the curriculum regime is necessary for LLATISA because it ensures to maintain balanced training across different cognitive levels, which single-stage training on high-level data fails to provide. L.2 Baseline Comparison We further evaluate ChatTime (Wang et al., 2025a) on the datasets we used across table 15 and explain the differences between TSRMs and Time-LLM (Jin et al., 2023). We clarify our evaluation results  (table 8)  and the architectural distinctions below: Evaluation on ChatTime. We evaluated ChatTime on our OOD benchmarks. As shown in table 8, ChatTime performs poorly across all levels. Notably, it fails to adhere to instruction-defined output formats (e.g., the index-value requirement for L1 tasks). Furthermore, as ChatTime only accepts single series, we had to manually concatenate multiple series and specify segment lengths to enable it to finish the Series Comparison (L3) task. Distinction with Time-LLM. LLATISA significantly outperforms its backbone model Qwen3VL-8B. This discrepancy highlights the necessity of our dual-view representation and multi-stage training on the difficulty-stratified TSR datasets. L.3 Expansion of VLM Baselines To provide more comprehensive evaluation, we have extended our benchmarks to include InternVL3-8B (Zhu et al., 2025) and Gemma312B (Team et al., 2025) across various visual input modalities. Following the same experimental settings introduced in Section 4.1, additional results are shown in table 9. Empirical results demonstrate that LLATISA consistently and significantly outperforms various VLM-based baselines of comparable parameter scale, regardless of the input modalities or representation strategies employed to numerical series. Notably, these newly incorporated VLM-based models exhibit similar performance scale to the Qwen3VL baselines reported in table 15, which all fall significantly short of LLATISA in both L1 numerical grounding and L3 semantic reasoning tasks. This further validates that our performance gains stem from both the specialized dual-view architecture and three-stage curriculum training paradigm.

You need first to check the overall pattern, then verify the precise values, and finally think whether the series exactly matches the given background knowledge. A: The time series shows periodic daily cycle with peak power outputs consistently occurring every 12th observation, specifically at index 12 (324.45 kW) and index 24 (1350.66 kW), reflecting regular wind patterns in Chinese wind farm.
